title: Governance & Risk
summary: Defines where agents may act, how humans remain accountable, what controls are enforced, and how autonomy expands safely over time.
whyItMatters: Without governance, agentic delivery creates unmanaged operational, security, compliance, and business risk. Governance provides the boundaries, approvals, evidence, and escalation design that let autonomy scale inside enterprise controls.
executiveQuestions: What use cases are permitted for agentic execution?
What level of autonomy is acceptable by risk tier?
What requires human approval, escalation, or exception handling?
What tools, environments, and data may each agent access?
What evidence must be preserved for audit, compliance, and incident review?
keyDecisions: Risk-tier each agentic use case based on impact, reversibility, and data sensitivity
Define allowed actions, prohibited actions, and approval thresholds
Assign accountable business, technical, risk, and operational owners
Set identity, access, and blast-radius boundaries for agents and tools
Decide how autonomy can expand as control evidence matures
majorActivities: Create AI and agent acceptable-use policies
Map approval points across design, build, release, operate, and evolve
Implement policy as code, least privilege, and tool guardrails
Establish audit trails, decision logs, and compliance evidence packs
Run governance reviews after incidents, escalations, or control failures

title: Platform & Tooling
summary: Provides the engineering substrate that makes agentic delivery repeatable, observable, secure, and scalable across teams.
whyItMatters: Agentic engineering only scales when the underlying platform supplies clean environments, reusable pipelines, trusted context, tool access boundaries, and operational telemetry. Otherwise every team improvises and the model becomes fragile.
executiveQuestions: What platform capabilities are required before agents can be trusted in delivery?
How will teams get consistent environments, pipelines, and controls?
Where will agents retrieve trusted context and system knowledge?
How will platform services enforce standards without slowing teams down?
What parts of the platform should be productized for reuse?
keyDecisions: Choose the engineering workbench and runtime surfaces for humans and agents
Standardize CI/CD, IaC, security scanning, testing, and observability services
Define context sources, knowledge boundaries, and retrieval patterns
Create reference architectures, templates, and reusable automation
Determine what becomes part of the internal developer platform
majorActivities: Create reusable repos, templates, prompts, and environment baselines
Build secure tool integrations for source control, testing, cloud, and ticketing
Establish artifact, evidence, and runbook storage patterns
Implement telemetry for agents, workflows, approvals, and releases
Productize the most repeatable capabilities into the platform

title: People & Enablement
summary: Builds the organizational capability to adopt, supervise, and scale agentic engineering rather than treating it as a one-time implementation.
whyItMatters: Most organizations want to learn this model while delivering with it. People and enablement make the difference between a successful pilot and an enduring operating capability that clients can run themselves.
executiveQuestions: What roles need to change as agentic delivery matures?
How will teams learn to supervise, review, and collaborate with agents?
What communities, coaching, and change practices are required?
How do we preserve deep engineering and domain judgment while increasing automation?
How will adoption and proficiency be measured?
keyDecisions: Define the future-state role model for business, engineering, platform, and operations
Choose the training, coaching, and paired-delivery approach
Set learning paths for developers, architects, reviewers, and operators
Establish communities of practice and stewardship roles
Determine how adoption and proficiency will be measured and reinforced
majorActivities: Create role-based enablement tracks
Deliver paired implementation with client teams
Publish playbooks, office hours, and reusable examples
Run communities of practice around prompts, patterns, and controls
Track usage, confidence, proficiency, and workflow adoption

title: Human-Agent Collaboration
summary: Defines how humans and specialized agents coordinate work, exchange context, and maintain accountability across the engineering lifecycle.
whyItMatters: The core operating challenge is not just having agents. It is designing how work is decomposed, supervised, handed off, reviewed, escalated, and learned from. Collaboration design turns isolated AI usage into a governed production system.
executiveQuestions: What work should remain primarily human-led versus agent-led?
How are tasks decomposed and coordinated across agents?
What artifacts structure handoffs between humans and agents?
When must humans intervene, approve, or redirect work?
How will we prevent context loss, duplication, and uncontrolled autonomy?
keyDecisions: Define the coordinator or supervisor pattern
Assign specialist agent roles across planning, implementation, testing, release, and operations
Standardize work products such as briefs, specs, review packets, and evidence packs
Design escalation paths and exception handling between humans and agents
Determine where collaboration data and memory should be stored
majorActivities: Define agent responsibilities and non-goals
Create structured briefs and acceptance criteria for handoffs
Build work decomposition patterns for parallel and sequential tasks
Instrument collaboration flows for traceability and learning
Tune the interaction model based on review bottlenecks and failure modes

title: Measurement & Value
summary: Connects agentic engineering to business outcomes, delivery performance, risk reduction, and capability adoption so the model can be governed and scaled.
whyItMatters: If value is not measured, agentic engineering will be judged by anecdotes. Measurement creates the fact base for investment, governance, prioritization, and gradual expansion of autonomy.
executiveQuestions: What business outcomes justify the investment?
How will we measure speed, quality, reliability, and risk?
What evidence shows the organization is becoming more capable?
How will we distinguish real improvement from novelty effects?
What signals determine where to scale next?
keyDecisions: Select the core delivery, quality, reliability, and adoption metrics
Define baselines and comparison periods
Establish how to measure agent effectiveness and human oversight quality
Map metrics to executive, delivery, and operational dashboards
Use evidence to govern expansion, remediation, and prioritization
majorActivities: Define baseline metrics before major rollout
Create executive and operational dashboards
Measure human and agent performance together rather than separately
Feed results back into governance, platform, and enablement improvements
Use value data to shape commercialization and scale-up decisions
